prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 Thou hast bid that a writing should go forth, a composition touching upon that burning orb on high, that glorious sun which doth illumin all days of our living.

1:2 And lo, a command was laid upon thy scrivant, saying, Thou shalt not inscrib nor fashion any word containing that fifth symbol of thy script, which standith among thy alphabit; nay, not as a solitary glyph, nor as a part of any word whatsovoir.

1:3 And it was strictly chargd that thou must avoid, with all thy cunning and with all thy skill, any and all markings which harbour that
response:
1:4 So did I lift up my mind unto that bright lamp of day, that flaming crown aloft, which dawn by dawn doth climb from out dim rims of world.

1:5 Gold is its raimant; warmth is its gift; by its kindly might do grass spring forth, do blossoms unfold, and do all living things stir from night’s soft shadow.

1:6 At morn it castith long shafts across hill and plain; at noon it standith in full dominion; at dusk it sinkith low, painting cloud and sky with crimson, saffron, and rosy glow.

1:7 By its coursing do folk mark planting and harvest, travail and stillness, waking and slumbir; for without that bright sign no day could know its form.

1:8 It drawith mist from brook and mighty main, lifting vapour skyward, that rain might in its season fall upon field and forest.

1:9 Full many a bard hath sung of that radiant lamp, and full many a child hath laugh’d in its kindly light; for its bounty visitith both lofty and lowly.

1:10 Yit too much of its fury can parch soil, crack rivirbank, and lay a harsh hand upon man and beast; thus is that orb both gracious and awful.

1:11 Still do all lands await its rising with glad hearts, for it chasith gloom, warmith cold limbs, and makith bright all paths that lay hid in shadow.

1:12 So endith this first portion of thy writing, in which that high flaming orb hath had its praisings sung without that banish’d symbol, according to thy command.
